narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 48% chance that you would not pass—high enough to trigger rejection as we prioritize admitting students with stronger projected success. This prediction drew from key aspects of your academic record, like your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and LSAT score of 31, alongside your middle family income bracket and full-time study plans. Among the factors the model weighed most heavily, your undergraduate GPA stood out as the strongest positive signal, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass since it's above the 3.24 average for students who succeeded on the bar. In contrast, your LSAT score had a notable pull in the opposite direction, contributing toward the risk of failure because it's well below the 37.27 average seen in passers. Your middle family income also nudged the outlook slightly toward potential challenges, as successful students more often come from upper-middle or high-income backgrounds—35% middle like yours, but 45% upper-middle and 8.5% high. Your Year 1 and Year 3 deciles of 4 were lower than the averages around 6 for bar passers, adding some concern, though they weren't the top drivers here. Compared to typical admitted students, who boast higher test scores and often stronger early rankings, your profile showed promise in GPA but gaps elsewhere that tipped the balance. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your solid undergrad performance and commitment to full-time study. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to the successful average, perhaps while seeking targeted prep that aligns with your background. Building connections through internships or bar prep programs could also highlight your resilience. Many students in similar positions improve their odds by addressing these key areas—reapplying after targeted growth often leads to success. You're already ahead with that GPA edge; channeling it strategically will make a difference.
